anchor.snippet: In particular, e 1 = e is transcendental.

annotations.label: Transcendence of e^α via second formulation

anchor.snippet: is linearly independent over the algebraic numbers and in particular e α cannot be algebraic
To prove that π is transcendental, we prove that it is not algebraic.

annotations.label: Transcendence of sin, cos, tan of algebraic numbers

anchor.snippet: if α is a non-zero algebraic number then sin(α), cos(α), tan(α) and their hyperbolic counterparts are also transcendental

anchor.value: Modular conjecture — Let q 1 , ..., q n

annotations.label: Lemma A implies e is irrational

anchor.snippet: Lemma A is sufficient to prove that e is irrational

annotations.label: Lemma A implies π is irrational

anchor.snippet: Lemma A also suffices to prove that π is irrational

annotations.label: Lemma B implies e is transcendental

anchor.snippet: Lemma B is sufficient to prove that e is transcendental

annotations.label: Lemma B implies π is transcendental

anchor.snippet: Lemma B also suffices to prove that π is transcendental

annotations.label: Transcendence degree reformulation

anchor.snippet: In other words, the extension field

annotations.note: Restates the theorem as: the field extension generated by e^{α_i} has transcendence degree n over ℚ.

annotations.label: Hermite's theorem (integer-exponent special case)

anchor.snippet: Charles Hermite first proved the simpler theorem where the α i exponents are required to be rational integers and linear independence is only assured over the rational integers

annotations.note: Special case of Lindemann–Weierstrass with rational integer exponents; the general result reduces to this case.
